title: Chapter 17: The Art of Being a Target
paragraphs: The road to Etcha, the last stronghold of the High Elves on this shore, was paved with white stone that was now hidden under a thick layer of gray, lifeless ash. Trees along the roadsides had long since turned into withered black skeletons. In this world, birds did not sing. The silence was broken only by the steady crunch of the footsteps of the four travelers.

They had been walking for several days already. Five years had passed since their first meeting by the campfire—five long, grueling years of wandering through the dying lands. Yashil was now twenty. He was no longer that frightened child who had been pushed into a pipe. His body had become wiry and resilient, and his face a calm mirror of the trials he had endured. In that time, he had bandaged Kirah's wounds hundreds of times, soothed Rene's melancholy, and restrained Dzhanavar's rage. He had become the core of this strange pack.

Kirah stepped with agonizing caution. Five years had passed, but he was still panicky about nearing the city—his roots in Zahan were too deep. Dzhanavar, on the contrary, strode broadly with his giant axe over his shoulder.
"Do you know what they say in the forest about long roads?" the werewolf yawned. "A wolf does not ask how far it is to the goal. But seven years on the road... that's a bit much even for me. Auf."
Rene walked slightly behind; the smell of her minty cigarette had become a familiar beacon in the mist.
"We are walking to certain death," the fox said melancholically. "Etcha has been locked for half a century. To them, we are just another portion of trash blown against their walls by the wind."
"If we do not come to them, they will just die thinking that only darkness remains beyond the walls," Yashil replied.

Soon the outlines of Etcha emerged from the mist. And not just the walls. At the foot of the colossal jade fortifications lay something unimaginable—the 'Grey City.' This was not part of elven architecture. It was a chaotic, sprawling labyrinth of tents, shacks, and dugouts that had grown over the years. Thousands of refugees, whom the elves refused to let in, clung to the city walls like parasites to the body of a dying god. Dwarves, half-orcs, hydrols—everyone the world had spat out into the darkness was here.

Yashil looked at this camp not with horror, but with recognition. Over the years, he had visited dozens of such places, always bringing not magic, but the simple ability to listen and share a last piece of bread.
The gate guards noticed them from a hundred paces away.
A sharp, guttural cry rang out. A wall of shields grew instantly on the walls. The captain of the guard in tarnished gold plate stepped onto a narrow balcony.
"Stop, scum!" his voice hit their ears. "One more step toward the Great Gates, and you will be made into pincushions for arrows!"

The elf looked at the approaching procession. His gaze shifted from the demon to the werewolf until it stopped on Yashil. The goblin stood in front, leaning on his faithful iron pipe, which had become smooth from his palms over seven years.
The captain, accompanied by guardsmen, stepped outside the walls through a small wicket. He approached closely, towering over Yashil.
The elf did not speak. He simply struck the goblin across the face with all his might. Yashil fell into the ash. Thick green blood splashed from a cut cheekbone.

"You are nothing!" the captain spat, kicking the prone Yashil in the chest. "You are filth that stains our walls! My brother died yesterday, and I will not let another portion of trash breathe our air!"
Kirah let out a rumbling sound, veins on his body flaring. Dzhanavar bared his teeth. But Yashil raised himself with difficulty.

"No, Kirah. Stand down," he commanded. The goblin rose slowly, not wiping the blood. He stood face to face with the captain again. "Your brother was killed by Zahan's warriors. But Zahan is a faceless horror. And I am right here. I am easy to hate."
Yashil stepped forward, baring his unprotected chest under the point of the elven blade.

"If my blood can muffle your pain—kill me. I will not resist. But when you are finished, look at these people in the camp. They are not your enemies. They just want what your brother wanted—to live another day."
The silence hanging over the gates of Etcha was deafening. The sword in the captain's hands faltered and slowly lowered.
"What are you?" the elf whispered.
"I am just a traveler who, in seven years, has learned that hatred is the heaviest burden one can carry," Yashil replied quietly.
The captain of the guard was silent for a long time. Then he turned to the wall.
"Lower the crossbows!" he commanded hoarsely. "I cannot let you into the city. But you can stay in the Grey City. You will not be touched."

He threw a flask of water next to Yashil. The goblin picked it up and, overcoming the pain, smiled. Over the next five years, Yashil transformed from a random newcomer into the living heart of this camp. The goblin who could not fight became the one who resolved bloody disputes between races, treated fevers, and found words of comfort where only emptiness remained. He organized communal kitchens, sharing the last of the grain, and protected the weak from marauders by the mere power of his conviction. For thousands of desperate souls, he became more than a friend—he became their last chance to remain human.
